stem: A specialty grocery store sells imported olive oil at $8.80 per liter. A customer wants to purchase 500 milliliters of olive oil. What is the cost of 500 milliliters? (1 liter = 1,000 milliliters)
explanation:
To find the cost of 500 mL, first convert 500 mL to liters, then multiply by the price per liter.

Step 1: Convert milliliters to liters.
$$500 \text{ mL} \times \frac{1 \text{ L}}{1{,}000 \text{ mL}} = 0.5 \text{ L}$$

Step 2: Multiply by the price per liter.
$$0.5 \text{ L} \times \frac{\$8.80}{1 \text{ L}} = \$4.40$$

The cost is **$4.40**.

distractorRationale.A: Choice A ($2.20) results from using an incorrect conversion of 1 L = 2,000 mL, so the student calculates $500 \div 2{,}000 = 0.25$ L, then $0.25 \times \$8.80 = \$2.20$. The student doubled the correct denominator, treating 500 mL as one-quarter of a liter instead of one-half.
distractorRationale.B: Choice B ($4.40) is the correct answer.
distractorRationale.C: Choice C ($8.80) results from using the incorrect conversion 1 L = 500 mL, leading the student to treat 500 mL as exactly 1 full liter: $1 \text{ L} \times \$8.80 = \$8.80$. The student uses the purchased volume as the conversion denominator, effectively making no conversion.
distractorRationale.D: Choice D ($17.60) results from inverting the conversion factor — the student treats 500 mL as 2 liters (dividing 1,000 by 500 instead of 500 by 1,000): $2 \text{ L} \times \$8.80 = \$17.60$. This is a reciprocal error in the unit conversion.
